chapter_summary: The sage Ruru, whose beloved wife (dear as his own life) had been killed by a snakebite, had vowed to slay every serpent he encountered, and so moves to kill a Dundubha snake. The Dundubha pleads that it is not one of the true biting serpents that harm humans — Dundubhas only resemble snakes by their smell — and that one who knows dharma should not kill the harmless along with the harmful. Persuaded and recognizing wisdom in its words, the frightened Ruru spares it, suspecting it to be a sage. Calming the creature, Ruru asks who it really is and how it came to this transformed state. The Dundubha reveals that it was formerly a rishi named Ruru, called Sahasrapat, reduced to serpenthood by a brahmin's curse. Ruru then asks why the angry twice-born cursed him and how long the snake-form will last.

story.title: Ruru and the Dundubha Snake
story.arc: Grief-driven vengeance meets discriminating dharma: Ruru, bereaved of his wife by a snakebite, has sworn to kill every serpent and raises his hand against a Dundubha. The Dundubha's plea — that the harmless must not be slain with the harmful, and that a dharma-knower discriminates — stays his hand. His mercy reveals the snake to be a cursed sage, opening the back-story of how a rishi named Sahasrapat fell to serpenthood by a brahmin's curse.
story.timeline_note: A framed dialogue narrated by Suta; the Dundubha's account of its former life as the rishi Sahasrapat is a flashback within the conversation.
story.comic_potential: A man on a furious snake-killing spree gets out-argued on ethics by the very snake he's about to brain — and then discovers it's secretly a sage who happens to share his own name, Ruru.

teachings.teaching: One who knows dharma must discriminate: the harmless are not to be destroyed along with the harmful. The Dundubhas, who do not bite humans, must not be killed merely because they resemble true serpents by their smell.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya framework, true discrimination (viveka) is the mark of the awakened: indiscriminate reaction born of grief or vow binds, while seeing each being as it truly is — separating the essential from the merely apparent — is the action of one established in dharma.

teachings.teaching: Beings that look alike may have wholly different natures, purposes, sorrows and joys; outward resemblance is not identity of essence.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma reads this as a caution against judging by form: the practitioner learns to perceive the inner reality (the consciousness within) rather than the surface appearance that the unawakened mistake for the whole.

teachings.teaching: When true knowledge arrives, even a vow taken in passion should yield to it: hearing the snake's wise words, the frightened Ruru withheld his blow, suspecting a sage.
teachings.lens_note: For Sharma, restraint at the moment of impulse — letting awareness interrupt the conditioned reaction — is the very practice that turns a reactive man into a seeker; the pause before the strike is where consciousness enters.

teachings.teaching: A curse can transform a being's very form: a rishi, Sahasrapat, was reduced to the body of a snake by a brahmin's wrath.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's view the descent of a luminous being into a lower form dramatizes how the consciousness, by a fall (here a curse), becomes encased in a grosser vehicle — and the narrative's promise of an eventual term to that form mirrors the soul's path back to its original state.
